subtopics.title: Attention Mechanism
subtopics.definition: Mechanism that weighs importance of input elements
subtopics.explanation: Attention allows models to focus on relevant parts of input when producing output. It computes a weighted sum of values based on query-key similarity. Scaled Dot-Product Attention formula: Attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(QKᵀ/√dₖ)V. Q (query) asks 'what to look for', K (key) represents 'what each element offers', V (value) is 'content to aggregate'. √dₖ scaling prevents gradient vanishing. Attention enables capturing long-range dependencies without recurrence. Applications: machine translation (align source-target words), image captioning (focus on relevant image regions), summarization (attend to important sentences).
subtopics.example: In translation 'I love cats' → 'J'aime les chats', attention aligns 'love' with 'aime', 'cats' with 'chats'. When generating 'aime', model attends most to 'love'.
subtopics.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
import numpy as np

class ScaledDotProductAttention(nn.Module):
    """Scaled Dot-Product Attention"""
    def __init__(self, dropout=0.1):
        super().__init__()
        self.dropout = nn.Dropout(dropout)
    
    def forward(self, query, key, value, mask=None):
        """
        query: (batch, seq_q, d_k)
        key: (batch, seq_k, d_k)
        value: (batch, seq_v, d_v)
        """
        d_k = query.size(-1)
        
        # Compute attention scores
        scores = torch.matmul(query, key.transpose(-2, -1)) / np.sqrt(d_k)
        
        # Apply mask (optional)
        if mask is not None:
            scores = scores.masked_fill(mask == 0, -1e9)
        
        # Apply softmax to get attention weights
        attn_weights = F.softmax(scores, dim=-1)
        attn_weights = self.dropout(attn_weights)
        
        # Apply attention to values
        output = torch.matmul(attn_weights, value)
        
        return output, attn_weights

# Demonstration
print("🔷 Attention Mechanism")
print("=" * 60)

attention = ScaledDotProductAttention()

# Example: Query, Key, Value
batch, seq_q, seq_k, d_k = 2, 3, 4, 64
query = torch.randn(batch, seq_q, d_k)
key = torch.randn(batch, seq_k, d_k)
value = torch.randn(batch, seq_k, d_k)

output, weights = attention(query, key, value)
print(f"\n📊 Attention Forward Pass:")
print(f"  Query shape: {query.shape}")
print(f"  Key shape: {key.shape}")
print(f"  Value shape: {value.shape}")
print(f"  Output shape: {output.shape}")
print(f"  Attention weights shape: {weights.shape}")
print(f"  Attention weights sum (per row): {weights.sum(dim=-1)[0][0]:.4f}")

print("\n✅ Attention Types:")
print("  • Self-Attention: Query=Key=Value (within same sequence)")
print("  • Cross-Attention: Query from one sequence, K,V from another")
print("  • Masked Attention: Prevents attending to future tokens")
print("\n💡 Key Properties:")
print("  • Parallel: All positions processed simultaneously")
print("  • Distance-independent: Direct connections between any positions")
print("  • Interpretable: Attention weights show importance")

subtopics.title: Self-Attention
subtopics.explanation: Self-attention computes relationships between all tokens in a sequence, allowing each token to attend to all others. For input X, compute Q = XW_Q, K = XW_K, V = XW_V, then SelfAttention(X) = softmax(QKᵀ/√d_k)V. This captures contextual relationships: 'bank' attends to 'river' or 'money' based on context. Benefits: captures long-range dependencies, parallel computation, interpretable weights. Self-attention is the core of transformer architecture, enabling understanding of word relationships regardless of distance.
subtopics.example: Sentence: 'The animal didn't cross the street because it was too tired.' Self-attention allows 'it' to attend to 'animal' (not 'street'), resolving pronoun reference correctly.
subtopics.code:
class SelfAttention(nn.Module):
    """Single-head self-attention"""
    def __init__(self, embed_dim, dropout=0.1):
        super().__init__()
        self.embed_dim = embed_dim
        self.W_q = nn.Linear(embed_dim, embed_dim)
        self.W_k = nn.Linear(embed_dim, embed_dim)
        self.W_v = nn.Linear(embed_dim, embed_dim)
        self.attention = ScaledDotProductAttention(dropout)
    
    def forward(self, x, mask=None):
        """x: (batch, seq_len, embed_dim)"""
        Q = self.W_q(x)
        K = self.W_k(x)
        V = self.W_v(x)
        
        output, weights = self.attention(Q, K, V, mask)
        return output, weights

print("\n🔷 Self-Attention")
print("=" * 60)

self_attn = SelfAttention(embed_dim=512)
x = torch.randn(2, 10, 512)  # (batch=2, seq=10, dim=512)
output, weights = self_attn(x)

print(f"\n📊 Self-Attention Forward:")
print(f"  Input shape: {x.shape}")
print(f"  Output shape: {output.shape}")
print(f"  Attention weights: {weights.shape}")
print(f"  Each token attends to all {weights.shape[-1]} tokens")

# Visualize attention pattern
print(f"\n📊 Attention Pattern Example:")
attn_sample = weights[0, 0, :].detach().numpy()
top_indices = np.argsort(attn_sample)[-3:][::-1]
print(f"  Token 0 attends most to positions: {top_indices}")

print("\n✅ Self-Attention Benefits:")
print("  • Captures relationships between any positions")
print("  • Parallel computation (unlike RNNs)")
print("  • Interpretable attention weights")
print("  • Handles variable-length sequences")

subtopics.title: Multi-Head Attention
subtopics.explanation: Multi-head attention runs multiple self-attention operations in parallel, each with different learned projections. Each 'head' captures different relationship types: syntax, semantics, coreference, positional. Outputs are concatenated and projected: MultiHead(Q,K,V) = Concat(head₁,...,head_h)W_O. Typically 8-32 heads. Benefits: richer representations, captures diverse patterns, improved performance. Heads learn specialized functions: some focus on local dependencies, others on long-range, some on specific word relationships.
subtopics.example: In 'The bank by the river', one head may focus on 'bank→river', another on 'bank→by', another on positional relationships. Combining heads gives comprehensive understanding.
subtopics.code:
class MultiHeadAttention(nn.Module):
    """Multi-Head Self-Attention"""
    def __init__(self, embed_dim, num_heads=8, dropout=0.1):
        super().__init__()
        assert embed_dim % num_heads == 0
        self.embed_dim = embed_dim
        self.num_heads = num_heads
        self.head_dim = embed_dim // num_heads
        
        self.W_q = nn.Linear(embed_dim, embed_dim)
        self.W_k = nn.Linear(embed_dim, embed_dim)
        self.W_v = nn.Linear(embed_dim, embed_dim)
        self.W_o = nn.Linear(embed_dim, embed_dim)
        self.attention = ScaledDotProductAttention(dropout)
    
    def forward(self, x, mask=None):
        batch_size, seq_len, _ = x.shape
        
        # Linear projections and reshape for multi-head
        Q = self.W_q(x).view(batch_size, seq_len, self.num_heads, self.head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        K = self.W_k(x).view(batch_size, seq_len, self.num_heads, self.head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        V = self.W_v(x).view(batch_size, seq_len, self.num_heads, self.head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        
        # Apply attention
        attn_output, attn_weights = self.attention(Q, K, V, mask)
        
        # Concatenate heads and project
        attn_output = attn_output.transpose(1, 2).contiguous().view(batch_size, seq_len, self.embed_dim)
        output = self.W_o(attn_output)
        
        return output, attn_weights

print("\n🔷 Multi-Head Attention")
print("=" * 60)

mha = MultiHeadAttention(embed_dim=512, num_heads=8)
x = torch.randn(2, 10, 512)
output, weights = mha(x)

print(f"\n📊 Multi-Head Attention:")
print(f"  Input shape: {x.shape}")
print(f"  Output shape: {output.shape}")
print(f"  Number of heads: 8")
print(f"  Each head dimension: {512 // 8} = 64")
print(f"  Attention weights shape: {weights.shape} (batch, heads, seq_q, seq_k)")

print("\n📊 Head Specialization:")
print("  • Head 1: Local dependencies (adjacent words)")
print("  • Head 2: Long-range dependencies")
print("  • Head 3: Syntactic relations")
print("  • Head 4: Semantic similarity")
print("  • Head 5: Positional patterns")
print("  • Head 6: Coreference resolution")
print("  • Head 7: Negation patterns")
print("  • Head 8: Discourse relationships")

subtopics.title: Positional Encoding
subtopics.explanation: Positional encoding adds information about token positions since self-attention is permutation-invariant (doesn't understand order). Two main types: 1) Sinusoidal encoding: fixed mathematical functions using sine/cosine of different frequencies, allows extrapolation to longer sequences. 2) Learned positional embeddings: trainable parameters per position, simpler but limited to max training length. Sinusoidal formula: PE(pos,2i) = sin(pos/10000^{2i/d}), PE(pos,2i+1) = cos(pos/10000^{2i/d}). Positional encodings enable transformers to understand word order, distance, and structural relationships.
subtopics.example: Without positional encoding, 'cat chased dog' and 'dog chased cat' have same representation. Positional encoding distinguishes position 1 (cat) from position 3 (dog), preserving order.
subtopics.code:
class PositionalEncoding(nn.Module):
    """Sinusoidal Positional Encoding"""
    def __init__(self, embed_dim, max_len=5000):
        super().__init__()
        pe = torch.zeros(max_len, embed_dim)
        position = torch.arange(0, max_len).unsqueeze(1).float()
        
        div_term = torch.exp(torch.arange(0, embed_dim, 2).float() * -(np.log(10000.0) / embed_dim))
        
        pe[:, 0::2] = torch.sin(position * div_term)
        pe[:, 1::2] = torch.cos(position * div_term)
        pe = pe.unsqueeze(0)  # (1, max_len, embed_dim)
        self.register_buffer('pe', pe)
    
    def forward(self, x):
        """x: (batch, seq_len, embed_dim)"""
        return x + self.pe[:, :x.size(1)]

class LearnedPositionalEncoding(nn.Module):
    """Learned positional embeddings"""
    def __init__(self, embed_dim, max_len=512):
        super().__init__()
        self.pos_embedding = nn.Embedding(max_len, embed_dim)
    
    def forward(self, x):
        batch, seq_len, embed_dim = x.shape
        positions = torch.arange(seq_len, device=x.device).unsqueeze(0).expand(batch, -1)
        return x + self.pos_embedding(positions)

print("\n🔷 Positional Encoding")
print("=" * 60)

pos_enc = PositionalEncoding(embed_dim=512, max_len=100)
x = torch.randn(2, 10, 512)
x_with_pos = pos_enc(x)

print(f"\n📊 Positional Encoding:")
print(f"  Input shape: {x.shape}")
print(f"  Output shape: {x_with_pos.shape}")

# Visualize positional encoding pattern
pe_visual = pos_enc.pe[0, :10, :10].detach().numpy()
print(f"\n📊 Positional Encoding Pattern (first 10 positions, first 10 dims):")
print(f"{'Pos':<5}", end="")
for d in range(10):
    print(f"{d:>8}", end="")
print()
print("-" * 85)
for pos in range(5):
    print(f"{pos:<5}", end="")
    for d in range(10):
        print(f"{pe_visual[pos, d]:>8.3f}", end="")
    print()

print("\n📊 Sinusoidal vs Learned Positional Encoding:")
print("-" * 50)
print("  Feature           | Sinusoidal      | Learned")
print("  ------------------|-----------------|------------------")
print("  Trainable         | No              | Yes")
print("  Extrapolation     | Yes (to longer) | No (limited)")
print("  Memory            | Low             | Higher")
print("  Interpretability  | High            | Low")
print("  Used in           | Transformer     | BERT, GPT")

print("\n✅ Positional Encoding Properties:")
print("  • Adds order information to permutation-invariant attention")
print("  • Sinusoidal: fixed, extrapolates to longer sequences")
print("  • Learned: trainable, simpler but limited length")
print("  • Enables understanding of distance and order")
print("  • Crucial for language understanding tasks")

print("\n📊 Complete Transformer Layer:")
print("-" * 40)

class TransformerBlock(nn.Module):
    """Single transformer encoder block"""
    def __init__(self, embed_dim=512, num_heads=8, ff_dim=2048, dropout=0.1):
        super().__init__()
        self.attention = MultiHeadAttention(embed_dim, num_heads, dropout)
        self.norm1 = nn.LayerNorm(embed_dim)
        self.norm2 = nn.LayerNorm(embed_dim)
        self.ffn = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(embed_dim, ff_dim),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(ff_dim, embed_dim)
        )
        self.dropout = nn.Dropout(dropout)
    
    def forward(self, x, mask=None):
        # Self-attention with residual
        attn_output, _ = self.attention(x, mask)
        x = self.norm1(x + self.dropout(attn_output))
        
        # Feed-forward with residual
        ff_output = self.ffn(x)
        x = self.norm2(x + self.dropout(ff_output))
        
        return x

# Complete transformer
print("\n📊 Complete Transformer Architecture:")
print("  Input → Positional Encoding → Transformer Blocks × N → Output")
print("\n  Each Transformer Block:")
print("    • Multi-Head Self-Attention + Residual + LayerNorm")
print("    • Feed-Forward Network + Residual + LayerNorm")
print("\n  Variants:")
print("    • Encoder-only: BERT (masked language modeling)")
print("    • Decoder-only: GPT (autoregressive generation)")
print("    • Encoder-Decoder: T5, BART (seq2seq tasks)")

print("\n✅ Transformer Summary:")
print("  • Attention: Focus on relevant information")
print("  • Self-Attention: Relationships within sequence")
print("  • Multi-Head: Multiple relationship types")
print("  • Positional Encoding: Order information")
print("  • Benefits: Parallel, long-range, interpretable")
print("  • Applications: NLP, vision, audio, biology")
